@isuruf I made one minor code change to fix a warning with Python 2.7. There should be no practical difference but let me know if you rebuild the wheels.

One last comment on the wheels, and Cython, and inter-operability ... I saw a discussion on SageMath regarding the use of statically linked versus dynamically linked extensions. My only concern in static vs. dynamic is ensuring that Cython code (probably equivalent to saying all-of-Sage) is using the GMP, MPFR, and MPC libraries as gmpy2. At the moment, the C-API is disabled with a static build of gmpy2. I don't mind enabling the C-API for a static build. Please let me know if you want me to make the change.

(MPFR/MPC could be tricky because the precision is a global variable. The current version of gmpy2 expects the precision to be set to the maximum for the calculation and then rounds the result down to the desired precision. During the rounding process, the existing precision is saved and then restored. If this is different than Sage's usage of the MPFR library, I'll need to change gmpy2 to always set the precision, at least on a dynamically linked build. I probably should do that anyway. Note that these concerns aren't applicable to GMP. I've already disabled changing the memory manager functions in GMP.)

_probably because aexit of the context manager waits for the tasks to finish._
yes it would wait for 1000 secs for all of the friend tasks and that is when parent calls `await kid_task.cancel()  `. At any await point, you must reckon with a `curio.CancelledError` exception. here the exception gets thrown and the try/except block of kid() would never get executed.

Lets forget about the name "Cancellation" for a second. We are talking about
a generic exception delivery mechanism to the running task.
But Cancellation has a special meaning for a task. (Cancelled status, etc..)
Should it be that way? If not what will be it relation to the timeouts.. ??
Why, what, how.. etc, etc...?

I think it is poor form in general to test whether two floats are equal, so maybe this is not such a problem. At any rate, in my opinion, it would be safest not to compare quantities that are not in the same units (including prefixes) although I can see this being something of a pain.  The advantage is that it would force the **user** to decide whether to convert q1 to q2's units or vice versa, instead of having some internal default based on ordering (say, always converting the second quantity to the same units as the first) that might surprise people who expect symmetry under the interchange of q1 and q2 but don't necessarily obtain it (rounding could work out differently, causing q1 == q2 to be true, but q2 == q1 to be false).

> I just uninstalled version 0.7.0 and did a pip install my mlxtend-0.8.0 ran the code changing the metric to "leverage" and it is giving me: ValueError: Metric must be 'confidence' or 'lift', got 'leverage'. Do you know why that might be?

Yeah, I haven't made an official release, yet. Since this is a bit of work, it's usually only done if enough features have accumulated to warrant the extra effort :P. So in this case, the 'leverage' would only be available through the master branch on GitHub (in version mxtend 0.8.1dev). You can install it, for example, using

    pip install git+git://github.com/rasbt/mlxtend.git


The list of features is kept here:

https://github.com/rasbt/mlxtend/blob/master/docs/sources/CHANGELOG.md

> Hmm, regarding Conviction being undefined in the special case that confidence = 1, can we exclude it at all such points in the algorithm where that is the case? What is typically done in algorithms where there is such a scenario? I would imagine this has to be a common case in the implementation of many algorithms because you will run into it whenever you have a formula with a divisor being able to equal 0.

I wouldn't exclude the point as they would have a very "good" score (i.e., 100% confidence). 

I did a bit more reading, and it seems that the co-domain or range of conviction seems to be 'officially' defined as [0, inf). So I think that using 'inf' in cases of zero division may be what's expected. I have to see how to implement this cleanly to avoid annoying 'zero division' warnings in the code.

Shared library size is usually not a big problem, because on all modern platforms shared libs are mmap-ed so data that is never used will not be physically loaded into memory. Actually the same is true for executables: they are mmap-ed, not loaded.
Size of static library is also not an issue, because it stays on developer size and during link phase only models that are actually used are linked into resulting binary.

I'm not sure if base64 effort worth it, because long binary data still takes time to compile while runtime penalties from compiled-in blobs are really negligible. But the choice is definitely up to :)

P. S. also note that splitting base64 string is inefficient because multiple calls to `std::ostringstream::operator<<` causes string growing with several memory reallocations, so it's more efficient to ask compiler (preprocessor actually) to concatenate strings for us.

@mpertierra, yes, I de-invert inverted relations by default. The Graph class is supposed to contain the DAG that the PENMAN serialization encodes. But if you consider the inverted edges as actual edges, then yes, there would be one root in the graph (indeed, this is a selling point of AMR/PENMAN).

In the most recent version (v0.6.0), I put more effort into making decode/encode round-trips return the same serialized graph, with just one or two exceptions. I do this by storing the observed edge orientation (inverted or true) as a secondary property on triples, and always preferring that orientation on serialization. Therefore it should be easy to detect if a triple was originally inverted or not. Is this sufficient for your application?

If not, it's possible to store inverted edges directly as triples (e.g. `(target, relation-of, source)`). You would create a subclass of PENMANCodec where the `handle_triple()` function does not do deinversion (alternatively, override the `is_relation_inverted()` function to always return `False`). I have not tested this, and it may not play nice with other parts of the code (e.g. `Graph.variables()`, possibly), but I think it's feasible.

@heitzmann : I am afraid that the problem that we are facing is considerably more complex. The fundamental flaw of the present offset() implementation is that it assumes that: 

OFFSET( polygon1 OR polygon2) == OFFSET(polygon1) OR OFFSET(polygon2). 

This is incorrect for adjacent or overlapping shapes. I have created an example to illustrate this. Please try zero and non-zero offset. You will observe that, even though the shape on the right is the same thing as the two shapes on the left, the outcome of the offset() algorithm leads to different results. This creates a failure opportunity for any shape that consists of numerous polygons.

```
import gdspy
import numpy

cell = gdspy.Cell('CELL')

pl1 = numpy.array([[ -1.,  0.], [0., 1.],  [0., 0.]])
pl2 = numpy.array([[ 0., 0.], [ 0., 1.], [ 1., 0.]])
pl3 = numpy.array([[ 2., 0.], [ 3., 1.], [4., 0.]])

cell.add(gdspy.Polygon(pl1))
cell.add(gdspy.Polygon(pl2))
cell.add(gdspy.Polygon(pl3))

offset_result = gdspy.offset(gdspy.CellReference(cell), distance=0.2)
offset_cell = gdspy.Cell('CELL_OFFSET')
offset_cell.add(offset_result)

gdspy.LayoutViewer(cells=[offset_cell])

```

Hello Bob,

Thank you for the contribution! It's always exciting to get support of any kind. I would be very happy to work with you to integrate what code you've written regarding the DIPPR methods. Do you just mean their numbered correlations, or property predictions methods? I have quite a few of both, but it's not exhaustive.

It's never fun to receive criticism, but there are some things I'd like changed with this PR. There are also things I like quite a lot in it. Here are all my comments, positive and negative.
* pyflakes suggests module imports should be before __all__, but pep8 disagrees (https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0008/#module-level-dunder-names) - I changed to pep8 a while back but used to do it the other way. I'm not sure why they disagree - do you know?
* The pylint directives at the top of files should be turned into a pylintrc for the project but some IDEs (including Spyder IIRC) don't use it unfortunately. There would be too much duplication putting it in each file.
* I'm not a fan of using `\` to break lines - I prefer to enclose the statement in brackets, mostly because I've forgotten the `\ `at the end of the line before and then the next statement gets executed but not as part of the previous expression and it's not assigned to anything. Not a deal breaker on the PR though. As an example, forgetting the second `\` silently breaks the following:
```
omega = log(101325.0/Pc) - 5.92714 + 6.09648/T_br + 1.28862*log(T_br) - \ 
                     0.169347*T_br**6)/(15.2518 - 15.6875/T_br - 13.4721*log(T_br) +
		      0.43577*T_br**6
```
* I developed an "internal" standard of having spaces between + and -, and not between / or *. I like your style of adding spaces where the groups between the signs are long such as indexing however. 
* ''' Internal function - TBD ''' is not helpful; I'd go for "Helper to generate a list of strings representing the available methods for the function" or something similar, if I were to document them. Otherwise it's hopefully self-explanatory and more importantly not user facing so not really requiring documentation.
* I really like the string concatenation you used on the error message of one of the "Exception" statements; Definitely something I'll probably adopt throughout my projects eventually and use in new functions for now.
* If you search for an import and it's not showing in the file, it's not used (unless the tests break, which I don't think will happen anywhere). Hard to keep track of without pyflakes turned on. I find it too much of a distraction while coding though. 
* All of the examples in the code are actually doctests, which get run automatically when py.test gets the argument "--doctest-modules". Not all of them are currently passing (in Chemical and eos/eos_mix only) so the those tests aren't being run automatically  right now. Each of the failures need to be investigated and occurred when I was moving faster than I should have. But, the ones you've changed by making the output take multiple lines will all fail because doctests inconveniently need to take. The inputs to the doctests don't have to be on one line but the next line continuing them needs to start with "... ".
* I hadn't been saving the project's files with the setting for spaces at end of lines to be removed; would you mind adding that setting to your PR? While the history is being broken for style anyway, what might as well be part of the push.
* The analytical solutions in the Rachford-Rice function were generated by SymPy. I appreciate you splitting them up; the reason I didn't initially though was that the easiest way to check it was correct was to re-generate the code. Not an issue to add line breaks now because it's working and is checked against the numerical solution in the tests too.

To recap, for this to be mergable, I'm asking for:
* Fixed doctests
* Go ahead and remove the unused imports
* I'd rather no documentation than "TBD"; "Undocumented - use at your own risk; subject to removal without notice" would work too. The functions in activity are mostly exposed to users through the Mixture class.
* Add a .pylintrc instead of the declarations
* Revert the import/`__all__ `switch or figure out why one is better than the other
* Remove spaces at the ends of each file with your IDE (don't worry about it if yours doesn't support it)

If you have specific questions about the code base, I can definitely answer them too. The core team is unfortunately just me at this point. The style changes are definitely appreciated. I'm quite interested and curious about what you've built but would appreciate more style changes if you feel like it. Feedback on the Chemical and Mixture APIs or property prediction methods would be appreciated too.

Thanks again for the interest in the project,
Caleb

I had a similar problem, where I have very many different compilers, and do not want to handle all variations in header-files while parsing,  so I use the fake headers. At the same time I want to use real headers when compiling  rewritten c-code.

I found https://github.com/akiradeveloper/macro-of-inline that uses pycparser to rewrite code. In cppwrap.py, function Apply, include-directives are restored to the output c-file by analyzing line-directives in preprocessed source (make sure to enable line-directives in preprocessor output), and also generating a separate AST for the included code that is then removed.

That version has some limitations, so I have an updated version that can handle mixing of comments/preprocessor-code and include-statements and also can handle superfluos includes, that the original cannot handle. It  is available in 
https://github.com/dmurdin/macro-of-inline (again in cppwrap.py/Apply). Note your code is still preprocessed except for include-directives so if you e.g. have preprocessor-macros with varying behaviour affected by includes, you could still have problems.

Would perhaps be nice if some write-c-with-restored-includes-function could be made part of pycparser proper.

I started writing an abstraction/interface for private keys (rsa, dsa, ec, ed, …) and hash functions.
The interfaces won't have artificial limits on what can be done, even if some operations are insecure or silly.

The actual crypto operations are implemented by optional backend libraries; there would be a small wrapper for each backend to make it compatible with my interface. There will be (optional) implementations for various paddings (e.g. PSS) in case a backend only supports a more basic operation.

It's supposed to be kind of like PKCS#11, but all python instead of C. Using "asyncio" where it makes sense and other modern python features like "typing".

Some backends I can think of:

  * cryptography
  * oscrypto
  * PyNaCl
  * textbook (still need a good name), **insecure** pure-python implementation of crypto primitives for testing
  * OpenPGP smartcard (I'll try to get my asyncio reimplementation for pyscard opensourced)
  * yubihsm 2
  * Your favourite cloud HSM
  * PKCS#11 (In case some HSM client cannot be rewritten in python)

There would only be few hard dependencies on other python packages, e.g. attrs and asn1crypto (for PKCS#1 1.5).

Other libraries could use mine to create digital signatures. E.g. build a TBSCertificate with asn1crypto, dump the DER, sign it, build the Certificate.

I'm not sure yet if this will be useful to others, it's currently more a plaything for myself and not yet ready to be made public.

If someone else worked on something similar in the meantime, please tell me so I can take a look before inventing my own wheel.

this time includes the full cycle of stopping all old and (re)starting all new containers. The overall deployment time depends on different things:
- the type of the application - I saw node.js containers wich take a very long time to get stopped. But nginx containers tend to stop immediately, etc.
- can old and new containers run in parallel (dynamic ports)?
- the deployment options and strategy (`Maximum percent` > 100)?
- the desired count of running tasks, compared to
- the number of ECS instances

If 600 sec works well for you, I don't see any reason why you should not use this value.

Best
Fabian

Serializing a task doesn't change where start and end messages happen; the remote messages in the second process are children. The way to detect start and end of action:

1. Start message is always number 1. So e.g. [1], [2, 1], [52, 353, 353, 1] are all start messages.
2. End message has action_type that matches message number 1 of that level. End messages that match the above might be [5], [2, 2], [52, 353, 353, 75] - they're always on same level 1.

So basically message number 1 is part of description of action, 2, 3, 4, 5, etc. are all children except for the last one that has matching level and is a succeeded/failed action message.

@kislyuk Thanks for reviewing. As I understand it, it's actually irrelevant whether you're using Python 2 or 3, since Bash determines the value of COMP_POINT by itself based on whatever encoding you're using for your shell. If you set _ARC_DEBUG you can see that COMP_POINT is the same either way, and always corresponds to the length of COMP_LINE in unicode characters.

As I mentioned in the issue, the Bash documentation says COMP_POINT is equal to `${#COMP_LINE}` when you are at the end of the line, and you can test that this works in unicode characters:

```
$ line='prog point test'
$ echo "${#line}"
15
$ line='prog point 你好嘚瑟 '
$ echo "${#line}"
15
```

Based on this, using `ensure_str(os.environ['COMP_LINE'])` seems like it's enough to make everything work as expected.

I think this issue should be reopened as spur does not really support SUDO commands that require passwords.  The problem is that there is no way to determine if the connection is waiting on a password from the user so there's no way to tell if you need to write the password to stdin.  This is an inherent problem with the fact that SshProcess() does not expose stdout until the process has completed.

One workaround is that you can run the following first to see if a password will be expected:
`ssh.run(['sudo', '-n', 'whoami'], allow_error=True).return_code`

That workaround is suboptimal though because sudo has a timeout for cached passwords so you never know when you'll run another command that needs you to resend the password.

I believe the issue really is that plumbum follows a non-standard return code policy. That is,
`false | true` has a return code of 0 in bash. Plumbum uses `or` to combine return code, making 0 special regardless of your requested return code.

I've set it so that 0 is expected for everything except the last command, which fixes the test that failed because it is the way plumbum has always worked. It just didn't attribute the non-zero command because it was a simple or, rather than the method of this patch.

If we can attach an expected return code to processes, that would be the ideal way to allow custom return code management. That should not be hard now that each command is properly tested.

It is not a bug in my opinion.

A vertex normal is defined as the (optionally normalised) weighted average of the face normals of the faces it is connected to.

When a vertex is not connected to any faces, this implementation will leave the normal at its initial value `(0,0,0)`. And, according to the definition, it really does not have a normal. So I think it makes sense.

We could treat this case differently, but really, I think it is out of scope for the Pyrr library.

So for your application all you need to do it make sure you compile and run with gcov enabled, have cpp-coveralls install, and then run cpp coveralls at the end to upload results

```
language: cpp
compiler:
  - gcc
before_install:
  - pip install --user cpp-coveralls

  - echo "do whatever you want in here to build and run your application/test"
  - echo "just make sure that you compile with gcov options"
  - echo "for gcc this means using the flags `-fprofile-arcs -ftest-coverage`"
after_success:
  - coveralls --exclude lib --exclude tests --gcov-options '\-lp'
```

I'm absolutely open to PRs to make the extra options simpler. (In fact, the pre-1.0 version of `ffmpeg-normalize` allowed this, since I used the `docopt` package for parsing options at the time, but it wasn't as flexible as `argparse`.)

The problem with Python's `argparse` is that it does not parse such “list of options” options correctly when they start with a dash. You can read about that [here](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/45541581/using-argparse-with-argument-values-that-begin-with-a-dash). That's why I went for a JSON list syntax.

One alternative would be to have people use the `=` sign.

You can evaluate the state of homeatic.devices with the same effect. For example:

```python
...
while not homematic.devices:
    time.sleep(1)
```
While no devices are created, homematic.deivces is an empty list. Lists (and many other objects) can be tested like bools. So an empty list is False, one with content is True. The desirend function would essentially do nothing else than ```return bool(homematic.devices)```.
I admit, that this way results in a race condition if this is tested while creation is still in progress.

If this is really needed, I would see the solution in adding a class-variable like "working", which is True while the device-creation is in progress, and set to False afterwards.

So in that case we could do
```python
...
while homematic._server.working:
    time.sleep(1)
```

Would that be a solution that fixes your requirement?

@regzhuce : Probably this could help you:

I finally could figure out how to use class weights for a (binary) classification problem though I still do not know how to achieve the functionality of a `mx.symbol.<...>Output`  layer to return the loss gradient and the prediction. However, here is my code to use a weighted version of cross-entropy when having two classes:

```R
# ... other layers, last layer's name is 'last_layer'
# Fully connected layer with 2 nodes
fc_last <- mx.symbol.FullyConnected(data=last_layer, num_hidden=2, name='lastfullyconnected')
# Label variable
label   <- mx.symbol.Variable(name='label')
# Softmax
softmax <- mx.symbol.softmax(data=fc_last, name='softmax', axis=1)
# Weighted cross-entropy
# label_weight in (0, 1), 1e-6 is added to avoid log(0)
nn_out  <- mx.symbol.MakeLoss(
    -1 * (1 - label_weight) * (1 - label)  * mx.symbol.log(mx.symbol.Reshape(mx.symbol.slice_axis(softmax, axis=1, begin=0, end=1), shape = 0) + 1e-6) -
              label_weight  *      label   * mx.symbol.log(mx.symbol.Reshape(mx.symbol.slice_axis(softmax, axis=1, begin=1, end=2), shape = 0) + 1e-6),
            name='weightedcrossentropy'
        )
```

After training, the same approach can be used to obtain predictions as described in [this example for a regression task](https://mxnet.incubator.apache.org/tutorials/r/CustomLossFunction.html).

To reproduce the setup I suggest looking at the jenkins function for
tutorial tests.

I had put a fix in my last PR before removing them from CI, the issue might
be gone already. Can someone start a few hundred runs of the tutorial tests
and see if it still happen? Note that they take ~25min so that could take a
few days.

Actually commenting out most tests except three very fast ones might be a
better idea since it isn't related to a specific test, and as one simple
test runs in 2-3s with the jupyter kernel overhead. To know which one is
fast check the tutorials, some do not much like the NDArray ones.

My current best guess is that the issue is related to the fact that the
ports used by jupiter internal mechanism are chosen randomly and that there
is linger=1000 hard-coded in the jupiter code somewhere that keep it being
used for 1sec. For every test there is ~1/10000 chance that the same port
will be reused (3 ports are picked between 1-100000), which makes it 1/300
because we have 30 tests and 1/150 because we run on python2 and python3.
That seems roughly consistent with the number of reports we've had, about
once every 150 CI runs.

There is no easy way to set the ports to fixed deterministic value. My
latest fix added a non-ideal 1.1 sleep between tests. Let's see if that
fixed it. The above explanation might be bogus too.

In a reply to my original question: The obvious place to look for missing image files is in the html file which is produced in the tmp directory. Look at the link to the image in the source code.

I found out that the link is the path set in the STATIC_ROOT-setting.

I read in an other post that STATIC_ROOT must be set, which is not the case in development.
Django looks for static files both in the app-directory and in cross-app STATICFILES_DIR directories.
STATIC_ROOT is a setting used for deployment and must be different from the other static directories, because it is used to collect static files from the other directories for convenience in deployment. (python manage.py collectstatic ) It is recommended that these files are served by a web server directly in production environment.

Perhaps this setting is used exclusively by the django-wkhtmltopdf script? If so, maybe this should be documented?

When I ensured that the image file was added to the folder referenced i STATIC_ROOT, it all worked out well.

However, I did find out that wkhtmltopdf indeed has a problem with svg-files. I had to convert it to a jpg for it to work.

@mrgleeco You may find direct use of GPG for file encryption to be more convenient for your use case. That said, try this:

1. Run `sops sshkey.yml` to create a new SOPS-encrypted file.
2. Add a top-level key called `ssh_key1` (the name can be anything, but you'll need it later).
3. Paste in the SSH private key. Make sure to use the `ssh_key1: |` syntax for a multiline YAML value.
4. Save and exit.

Then you can call sops with the `--extract` option to retrieve just the value:

```
ssh -i <(sops -d --extract '["ssh_key1"]' sshkey.yml | tail +2)
```

The `--extract` option lets you select a single var. The `| tail +2` omits the first line of output, which is `|`, given the multiline YAML syntax mentioned above.

Hope that helps!

Generated trie based language models using _separately_ the data sets LibriSpeech, VoxForge, and English Wikipedia.

Instead of creating a single trie model the parameters defining the model were varied. For example the n-gram depth, pruning, vocab estimate, array, quantization, bit depth...

Through all of these variations the tries produced by the English Wikipedia text, an 11GB text, were always greater than the current 1.48 GB language model. So they would not result in a size win. Thus were not further pursued.

For the LibriSpeech and VoxForge tests were made against the librivox test clean all with the same acoustic model to determine which language model performed best. Results are here[[1](https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/10iccPZKrTK2-tfSHgDdoIXGXY3vRbAw1im_rQ0KOlhI/edit?usp=sharing)].

> I'm also running into errors when I try to rename using the lsp-mode in emacs.

This appears to be a result of a) how line numbers get serialized and b) the types that `lsp-mode` expects. For rename, pyls is returning `sys.maxsize` for the end line, and - at least when it gets to emacs lisp via stdout - that values is serialized in "scientific notation" (e.g. 9.2e+18). `lsp-mode` ultimately passes this to `forward-line` which expects and integer but, unfortunately, this is a floating point value.

Yeah, but current_process here is set to `self` and self should be `Process` instance, not `Worker`.

The Worker object is used as the target for the process, and then it copies some attributes over (like inqueue fd, etc, that the celery async pool uses to keep track of things):

https://github.com/scrapy/scrapy/blob/master/scrapy/utils/log.py#L98-L101

So why is `_current_process` a `Worker` instance, and not a `Process` instance?

I propose the following modifications and/or bug fixes:
* All `prints` and commented lines of code (some were found in `owpreprocessor.py`) should be deleted.
* When `None` is selected in `Stop word remover` it causes a unnecessary `NoneType object is not subscriptable` warning.
* `Lowercase` checkbox IMO fits better into the `Stemmer` section than into the `Tokenizer`.
* Until `twitter tokenizer` is implemented, please gray/comment it out from the GUI.
* I suggest removing the `tokenizer type` and `stemmer type` labels from the GUI, since the title of the section tells what this is about.
* Should we rename the `word tokenizer` option to `NLTK tokenizer` or something similar, since `twitter tokenizer` is also a word tokenizer, just adopted for tweets?
* The sections could be renamed to `Tokenization`, `Stemming` or even better `Normalization`, and `Filtering` or `Token Filtering`.
* It would be awesome if we implemented progress bar, since Lemmatization can take quite some time.
* Selecting different stopwords file does not trigger a commit when autocommit is enabled.
* A reload button for stopword files would be very nice.
* We could enhance the `Stopword removal` with the option to remove tokens by document frequency`min_df` and `max_df` which are either `float` of `int` like in [scikit](http://scikit-learn.org/stable/modules/generated/sklearn.feature_extraction.text.CountVectorizer.html).

About the order of preprocessors it might be better if tokenizer is always on top. Also, since we support different order of `Stemmer` and `Stopword removal` this should either be implemented in scripting part (to support custom order) or we should disable the support for custom ordering in the widget to prevent confusion.

Thanks for the suggestions @perrygeo, I chose to use iso3166's country code list for validation.

One thing that's worth mentioning is that the Mapbox API doesn't support every country in this list so there may be cases where the API will return an error message such as this:

```{u'message': u'Stack "zw" is not a known stack. Must be one of: cn, au, nz, sg, br, ca, at, de, es, fr, nl, gb, ie, us, ao, bw, dz, eg, ke, ma, za, ae, bh, bn, id, in, jo, kw, my, ph, th, tw, vn, ad, al, ba, be, bg, by, ch, cy, cz, dk, ee, fi, gr, hr, hu, is, it, lb, lt, lu, lv, md, me, mk, mt, no, pl, pt, ro, rs, ru, se, si, sk, sm, tr, ua, jp, ar, cl, co, cu, gf, gp, mx, pe, uy, ve'}```

This error message is actually where I had gotten the original validation tuple from ('zw' is CC for Zimbabwe BTW).

My thoughts are that it's probably best to use the iso3166 package for a quick sanity check and then if the user provides a country code not supported by Mapbox they will have to handle the API error like any other they might receive. In my opinion this is better than having to maintain a separate country code list that could easily get out-of-date.

@chetan51 cc @scottpurdy I faced the same problems recently and discovered that this revolves around the SWIG C/C++ pre-processor. I copied your four changed files into a personal branch, made changes, and got this working on Windows locally. See this for a comparison of my changes with Numenta master - 
https://github.com/numenta/nupic.core/compare/master...rcrowder:issue-674

To get this work you need to change to using ```#if !defined(CAPNP_LITE)``` (do not remove any dollar signs).

Why: At first, like you tried, I had ```-DCAPNP_LITE``` on the SWIG calls, but the pre-processor macro expansion substituted this which made the _cxx.cpp files contain ```#if 0``` (it assumes it expanded to ```-DCAPNP_LITE=0```). Setting it to =1 made similar problem. Dollar sign helps tell SWIG pre-processor to do something different in the ```.i``` files. And a main reason the pre-processor definition with SWIG commands is now ```-DCAPNP_LITE=CAPNP_LITE```.

NOTE: The Asynchronous Input/Output networking part of Capnp in the KJ library is only for Linux. Windows handles this differently and looks like no one has helped add the Windows way to capnp. Hence the need for CAPNP_LITE to guard that part of KJ. Builders and readers, using dynamic.h and schema-parser.h etc code in capnp, a reason PyCapnp.hpp is not used on Windows (guards in PyRegion.cpp), and difficulties with pycapnp in Python on Windows. We've found that this is also the case with GCC for Windows (missing poll.h that you saw recent with this work).
Lots to bear in mind, hence the CAPNP_LITE guards in lots of places. Something that may not change for quite a while.

Sorry if I wasn't clear in my explanation before but I have interpreted it as you have said and based on what I've seen and tried previously, I do not believe it would work. From my understanding, Django writes the entire encoded session dict to the database at the end of the request, regardless of whether you have manually changed the last activity information in that request or not. PASSIVE_URL only prevent session_security from processing the request, but it does not prevent the regular Django session handling. Therefore, session_security's last activity information will get overwritten with stale data at the end of the long request, because the entire session data is overwritten.

Now I have not tested this hypothesis directly, so I am not 100% certain this is the case, but at the time I encountered this problem, I did a bunch of testing on this issue and this was my conclusion. It seems to be an inherent problem in the way Django handles the saving of session data and the only way I can think of to fix this is to somehow tell Django to update the session data to/from the database periodically before the end of the long request so that it will share the data more nicely with other concurrent requests. I believe the root of this issue is actually a concurrency problem.

I don't have any qualms about this issue being closed as I have worked around it, but it could still be an issue for others. But from the lack of activity on this issue, it looks like this is a rare problem that no one else encountered. It would probably be a good idea to document this known problem somewhere if you wish to close this issue.

So, for some reason, your version of numpy is not respecting the command `np.set_printoptions(threshold=np.inf)`, which is supposed to prevent it from doing all those `...` in the array reprs.  I'm not sure that figuring out a workaround for this is the best use of my time.  

Any chance you can just upgrade your numpy version?  It's pretty easy to do.  `pip install -U numpy`.  Add `--user` if you don't have root privileges.

For the question about non-frozen flow, it's easy to get repeated realizations of the atmosphere.  Just duplicate the rng at the start of a phase generation.  Then every time through you would get the same series of phase screens.  And I think this is what we want the default behavior to be.  Calling `atm.getPSF()` repeatedly with different positions should get the same realization of the atmosphere but at different locations on the exposure.  

We could also have an option to continue on from the end of the previous time series, but that should at least not be the default behavior, since most of the time, users will be working with a single exposure, which had a single realization of the atmosphere over the exposure time.

I think for the API on this, we want an Atmosphere object to logically define the state of the atmosphere at the start of an exposure.  Then every time you generate a PSF from it, it integrates forward for the given location.

For the implementation, one way to do this would be to have Atmosphere store the rng it was provided on construction as `rng_orig` and use it directly.  Then when making a new PSF, make a copy of it: `rng = rng_orig.duplicate()` and make a fresh AtmosphericPhaseGenerator with the copy.

This has the advantage that an Atmosphere object is logically immutable.  It refers to the atmospheric conditions at a particular moment in time.  So repeated builds of PSFs are deterministic.  

However, I can imagine wanting to build a time series of sequential atmospheric PSFs.  So a couple ideas about an API for that:

1. later_atm = atm.timeSkip(nseconds)  # Skip nseconds into the future
2. next_atm = atm.continuation()  # Get an atmosphere that corresponds to the end of the last PSF generated.

Option 1 preserves the immutability model better, since there would be no internal state that matters for anything the user can see.  But it would require discarding a rather large number of random numbers.  I think that's ok.  Discarding 10^8 values takes much less than 1 second, so it's pretty fast.  (You can cherry-pick the commit where I added the discard function here: df42a810)  But as nseconds gets large relative to the time step, this could become a significant operation.  You'd also have to be careful about figuring out exactly how many values to discard.  We'd want to design a good unit test for this.

Option 2 might be a bit more efficient, but it would require some extra work in the AtmosphericPhaseGenerator class, since we'd want to be able to return the current state and be able to construct a new Atmosphere from that (rather than vice versa).  

All in all, I think I prefer option 1.  I doubt the discarding of rng values will ever be slow enough to care about.

Some comments about the changes to getPSF:
* I never really liked the dict return value, so I'm fine with getting rid of that.  Even if this isn't backwards compatible.  (It's less important I think for submodules to strictly keep backwards compatibility.)
* I think would go another step and just have getPSF only ever return a single PSF.  So `SCA` needs to be a single number, and the return value would always be a single PSF object.  It looks like the only efficiency concern to this is that you currently do a fairly complicated bit to reuse PSFs that are identical except for the bandpass but have the same pupil plane type.  I think this is something we can just leave to the user and just tell them about `galsim.wfirst.longwave_bands` and `galsim.wfirst.shortwave_bands` (or provide a helper function front end to them if you prefer) and let them reuse the PSF objects by hand.  I think this will normally fit easily into their code and would get rid of some complicated bits in getPSF.
* I would add 'long' and 'short' as allowed values for the `bandpass` parameter to help point out the likely efficiency gain to be had from just building two PSF objects for each SCA if you are planning to draw all the bands.
* To aid in transition, we could have the default bandpass be something other than None (say 'undefined') and provide a useful error message if the user doesn't give the bandpass, telling them why they need to and what the new API is.  Add a comment that this bit can be removed when we get rid of 2.x deprecations.

> So, an ideal situation would be where I can run my code in isolation while excluding the other top-level fields.

This doesn't make any sense.  If you don't want to do anything with the other top level fields, then why would you want to run this using the `galsim` executable?  

The `galsim` executable is a very specific python program that does particular things with the yaml file.  It sounds like you want to do very different things with your yaml file, so you should just write you own python code that does what you want and takes the yaml file as an argument.

At Stripe, one of our core observations was that the little details are what changes an API from acceptable to actually a pleasure to use. (And particularly, the most important details happen during interactive development: when you're first `curl`ing around a new API. We would do things like pre-fill your test API key in the API docs, so you can just copy-paste the examples directly without having to figure out where your API key lives, etc.)

Certainly developers can pipe the output of a curl to `jq`. But even better would be anticipating this use-case and returning the JSON already in a human-friendly format.

Regardless of the philosophy, this framework already does `indent=2` in one case; here I've made it consistent and added trailing newlines. I'd also be equally happy if this functionality were easily pluggable. But I think it's actually a pretty good thing for the framework to provide little finishing touches that otherwise most people probably wouldn't bother with.

C'est bon merci @fpagnoux !

About weeks, this is going to bring tricky questions about _relationship between periods_.

Right now, OpenFisca period model relies on the assumption that period fit well together. A `YEAR` is composed a 12 `MONTH`s . We could relatively easily add `DAY`, as a month is composed of 28 to 31 `DAY`s. <sub><sup>We actually used to support them, but deprecated it as no one was using it</sup></sub>.

However, `WEEK` is more challenging: they don't fit well in a month, nor in a year. And question will arise:

- If `A`'s definition period is the week, can I calculate `A` for a year?
- If so, what do I do with the weeks that are only partly in the year? 
- Can we have a _generic_ answer to the previous questions, or is it going to be _country-specific_?

These are really interesting and important discussions.

I think one idea that pushed towards using singular variable names was an attempt to partially _hide_ the vectorial complexity to the user. The idea being: 
> "Many calculations will work as expected without worrying too much about what `person` _is_ exactly. Let's not bother the user with that, _until_  they reach more complex usecases where they will need to understand more about the internal vectorial nature of the computations."

What I'm hearing from different sources here is that this idea doesn't really work, because users are _very quickly confronted_ to these more complex use cases. So it would be better to be honest about it from the beginning, and not try to hide that they are handling vectors.

Il me semblerait plus pertinent que le modèle socio-fiscal soit le plus proche possible de la législation, et donc que cette valeur reste un entier.
Néanmoins, il est tout aussi légitime que la donnée d'entrée soit fournie, dans le cas présent, sous la forme d'une valeur décimale : la _réalité_ des frais réels est décimale, la _représentation législative_ est entière.

Il me semble donc clair qu'ici l'API d'OpenFisca (pas seulement l'API web, l'API python) devrait accepter un flottant pour une valeur entière, l'arrondir et possiblement notifier qu'un arrondi a été appliqué.

Deux possibilités d'implémentation s'offrent à nous :

1. Arrondir dans le code de la formule.
2. Fournir une information déclarative comme métadonnée de la formule, qui ajoute le prétraitement par arrondi directement via `core`. Cette déclaration devra supporter les trois types d'arrondis existants (`floor`, `round`, `ceil`).

J'ai créé https://github.com/openfisca/openfisca-core/issues/463 en conséquence, afin d'implémenter la solution 2, qui est généraliste.
D'ici à ce qu'elle soit implémentée, je propose d'ajouter l'arrondi dans le code de `frais_reel`.

> Use asynchronous speech recognition to recognize audio that is longer than a minute and **stored in Google Cloud Storage.**

I converted (mp3) audio files to raw and uploaded to Google Cloud Storage.


Here's the code - 


```
import subprocess, os, io
from google.cloud import speech
from google.cloud.speech import enums
from google.cloud.speech import types


def transcribe_gcs(gcs_uri):
    client = speech.SpeechClient()

    audio = types.RecognitionAudio(uri=gcs_uri)
    config = types.RecognitionConfig(
        encoding=enums.RecognitionConfig.AudioEncoding.LINEAR16,
        sample_rate_hertz=16000,
        language_code='en-US')

    operation = client.long_running_recognize(config, audio)

    print('\n\n\n')
    response = operation.result(timeout=9000)

    for result in response.results:
        print('{}'.format(result.alternatives[0].transcript))

import time
t = time.time()
gcs_uri = 'gs://voicedatainfy/async7.raw'
transcribe_gcs(gcs_uri)
print("TOTAL TIME TAKEN: ", time.time()- t)

```

**Take care** while 
- passing arguments to config - encoding and sample_rate_hertz, and
- gcs_uri.

LogiclaIdForSimpleTable:

      PrimaryKey:

      ProvisionedThroughput:

The main issue isn't with exhaustively listing contributors or not, but that it isn't clear that all the contributors have actually licensed their contributions under the appropriate licence.

It's a rather annoying situation in which we can't put a license on the project until we confirm that the contributions are licensed as that, but until we do we'll need to get an explicit licence stated for contributions we merge (or else make the situation worse).

@omron93 I'm not a copyright lawyer, but I'd think an email, or a comment on the PR in which the code was submitted, from them stating that they hold the copyright on their contribution and that they agree to license it under the 2-clause version of the BSD licence as described at: http://www.opensource.org/licenses/bsd-license.html

If anyone isn't the copyright holder (for example, if it's owned by their employer) they'll need to get agreement of the copyright holder.

If anyone doesn't agree, I think we probably have to just drop their contribution.  There haven't been a huge number since moving to github, and for those I've merged I think I've asked about licensing in the PR before merging.

Thanks for looking into this.

Sorry for the confusion on the earlier thread.

1. `mounts` and `binds` are 2 different things, as explained [here](https://docs.docker.com/storage/bind-mounts/) (in the context of the CLI). `mounts` is more modern and a better choice 99% of the time.
2. Because of the way the `binds` API was designed, `{'/test:dir': {'bind': '/test'}}` has to be transformed into `['test:dir:/test']`, which the daemon fails to parse (as it expects the colon to be a path separator). This is not a SDK issue. ([See here](https://docs.docker.com/engine/api/v1.33/#operation/ContainerCreate) for the Docker daemon API)
3. As @thaJeztah pointed out, [the correct approach to this problem is to use `mounts` instead](https://github.com/docker/docker-py/issues/2041#issuecomment-392261637)

```python
import docker
c = docker.from_env()
mnt = docker.types.Mount(type='bind', source='/tmp/colon:colon/', target='/colon')
ctnr = c.containers.create('busybox', mounts=[mnt]) # <Container: 38869806cb>
```

HTH

@benwilber Pretty much, but the final step where the Node.js API calls the PHP API is done with the FQDN as Deis does not yet have internal service discovery. Our API calls are always made to the FQDN over HTTPS, so the route would always be secure and our platform can follow 12 factor methodologies as closely as possible.

As an example the application makes it's call to `https://api.example.com`, that API sees the requested route legacy and in turn makes a request to `https://apiv1.example.com`. This works 100% with `enforceHTTPS` set to `false`.

Based on the route in the flow would look more like this...

    ELB (tcp/443) -> Deis router (https/443) -> nodejs API (http/80) -> ELB (tcp/443) -> Deis router (https/443) -> PHP API (http/80)

So we never make a non-SSL request ever.

Let's close this in favour of more tightly-scoped issues which we can reasonably address. Glad to see that the router's more secure though.

Interesting.  I think I see what is happening.  The *ancestor* and the *uncle* are both at the same height.  What I think we are seeing is a chain re-org where the existing chain `HEAD` block is being re-organized to be an uncle.

We'll need to update that validation logic to take this situation into account.  Currently we pull ancestor hashes by looking up the canonical block for a given number.  I think we need to change this to instead trace back through the parent hashes because during block validation we only actually care about the chain of blocks that the new block is part of, not the canonical chain.

> this looks like a user error.

Unfortunately, I didn't even make this mistake myself. It's exactly what happens when you use Conda with readthedocs.org:
- read the docs installs your desired environment from a file with `conda env`. In my case, this uses conda-forge.
- read the docs then installs a few unrelated dependencies (e.g., their Sphinx theme) with `conda install` from the default channel.

I'm not sure how the second command could be aware of the channels used to setup the environment, but if that's possible I would gladly fix this for read the docs.
No need to reassign, @msarahan, I've got this.

> Conda will downgrade explicitly installed packages in order to update implicitly installed packages (dependencies of the explicitly installed packages)

Indeed, it will, with the `--all` flag.  We don't right now incorporate knowledge of explicitly installed packages into update `--all`, but I agree it's something we should do.  Along with https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/4394

@ilanschnell I just tried @asmeurer's #1133 and it doesn't solve the problem that this PR does.  Here's the test scenario that @jreback showed me.

* Create an environment with pandas 0.14 in it
* Launch a python interpreter and import pandas then leave the interpreter open
* In another cmd session, run `conda install pandas=0.16*` and when prompted to force the installation, force it

At this point, everything looks as those its fine until you try to run `import pandas` and then you have some scripts trying to import things that were silently ignored during the previous update.

I know this code is far from perfect, but it does what it's intended to -- keep you from hosing an installation the way that we've had reported a bunch of times.  I think it's pretty solidly on the 80 side of the 80/20 rule.

Am I missing some possible bad side effect from stopping the unlink for a package before it starts unlinking?

I think this issue should be re-opened because, as far as I can tell, no where is it documented that the --custom-data parameter supports both a file name or plain file content, nor is the "@" strong gesture documented (again, as far as I can tell.)

I looked both here https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/virtual-machines/linux/using-cloud-init and here https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/cli/azure/vm?view=azure-cli-latest#az-vm-create .  Both pages imply that only a file name is valid; neither mentions plain file content nor the "@" parameter.

So at a bare minimum, this behavior should be clearly documented.  But ideally, the cli should be more intelligent.  I still think it's bad behavior to have a parameter which can fail silently.

One suggestion might be to have the cli assume that the parameter passed to --custom-data is a filename, unless it begins with a hash (#), which would be an indicator that you were passing file content.  (Yes, I know it's the case that cloud init doesn't *require* a comment at the beginning, but you could make that a requirement of the cli and it wouldn't really hurt anything.)  This would also make it easy to additionally support urls in addition to file paths as well as file content.

@yugangw-msft any comment or suggestions here?

> What I need is a clear distinction of what packages can be shipped together and which not.

Any packages that depends on msrestazure 0.4.x can be shipped together without any problem (roughly everything released after June 2016)

> As far as I know, PyPi can mark packages as "pre-release". So, what I would suggest is to not mark packages as stable in PyPi until you have updated enough packages to release a new version of the meta packages.

Problem is, when I release `azure-mgmt-compute`, **it is** stable :/. I have users that just care about this particular package and don't use `azure` or `azure-mgmt`, and using a preview package won't be fair for them.

I just got a long discussion with @brettcannon about this about the `azure` story. We think that by essence there is no good way to maintain a semantic versioning of a meta-package like `azure`. We should see this package as a quickstart alias to simplify the initial setup. Trying to pin versions inside just does not work, and creates regular problems.

Our current suggestion is then to stop pinning version in `azure` and assume his alias role (this is mostly what people asks from us actually). This means:

- We release `azure` 3.0 with no pinned dependencies. Azure 3.0 is a an alias list and nothing more.
- We release `azure` 3.(x+1) versions only on new packages coming.
- We don't planned 3.x.y release (bug fix release)
- We guarantee strict semantic versioning at the service level
- Then, the correct way to pin your app, is to `pip freeze` the service dependency, not `azure`. Note that already today, `pip freeze` would have give you specific version of packages anyway.
- Packages that are not considered stable (meaning: we expect regular breaking changes) are not in `azure` and must be installed by name.
- Packages in `azure` are considered stable enough, so we don't plan a lot of breaking changes releases.
- We don't touch to `azure` 2.0, since we took the commitment that it will never breaks (hence 3.0)

OK I have addressed most comments. I talked more with Chris, Jason and Sylvain about the action name prefix naming.

* In the long run, if needed, we can provide a end-user-human-readable version of the command namespace for usage in UI elements.
* For this PR, I mostly want to get the programmatic one right.
* The feeling here is that the command namespaces should roughly follow JS package names. From this perspective, the notebook package will be `jupyter-js-notebook` so the command prefix would make sense as `jupyter-notebook`.
* Also feeling here like they should be verbose (more verbose than CSS) and developer-human-readable, so `jp-notebook` is too opaque.
* These will be scoped at the level of major components in the UI (what we are calling plugins on the phosphor side of things). When all is said and done, there will tons of plugins, many of which we don't develop. Because of this, I think it will be very useful to have the extra `jupyter-` in the namespace to group our components together:

`jupyter-texteditor`
`jupyter-terminal`
`jupyter-debugger`
`jupyter-filebrowser`
etc.

Based on these discussions, the current state of the PR is to use `jupyter-notebook`.

As an aside, I think it would be helpful to use a different name (maybe the shorter `jp-notebook`) for our CSS prefix to more easily allow searching through our codebase for developers.

Thoughts?

way of handling this would be some sort of syntax within the cells,
maybe that taps into the multiline magic ideas:

%mycell
class Foo(object):

...a Markdown cell or so...

%mycell
    def **init**(self, a):
        self.a = a

It would be trivial to piece together and execute the union of these.
It also keeps the actual UI complete clean and simple.  We could also
use this to introduce other grouping symantics.  The downside is that
it clutters up the code cells themselves.

is based on the heading grouping.  In some ways that is the simplest
way of going and would require no additional UI of cell clutter.

I think this would make the notebook extremely literate - more so than
any environment I have seen before.

> The one thing where even the notebook doesn't work very well if you want to mix text-based explanations with code, is the definition of complex objects.  The reason is a limitation imposed by Python itself: Python does not allow you to declare a class in multiple execution chunks (well, you can, but only using dynamic hacks that aren't very readable and which I will henceforth ignore).

> This means that if we start using the notebook for declaring more complex classes, right now they have to live in a single, long cell.  This prevents you for example from inserting a text paragraph with a mathematical explanation before continuing with the next method.
> 
> It occurred to me that if we had a concept of a 'cell group' where all the cells in the group would be _unconditionally_ executed _always together, all of them_, then we'd solve this problem.  Consider creating a cell group as stated above, then when you hit Shift-Enter from any cell in the group, we actually send for execution the union of all the code cells in the group ignoring non-code ones.  Then, Python would always see them as a single cell, while the human could break up the visual layout between code and text as best fit the problem.
> 
> There are some UI challenges in how to best do this, but the more I think about it the more I like this idea.  It would make the notebook a very nice environment for literate programming in a way that no existing python tool allows.  Thoughts?
>

That is a good argument @nri-pl,  although backwards compatibility has to be considered a little bit and also `read`/`recv` do not behave the same way and have different exceptions to handle. You can also achieve the same desired functionality with a small wrapper class, e.g. (Python 3):

```python
from collections import namedtuple
from lz4framed import compress, Decompressor

# Present any function as a class with a read function
ReadWrapper = namedtuple('nt_ReadWrapper', 'read')

# Then can e.g. do:
for chunk in Decompressor(ReadWrapper(socket.recv)):
    print(repr(chunk))

# or you could even subclass the Decompressor class, e.g.:
class SocketDecompressor(Decompressor):

    def __init__(self, socket):
        super().__init__(ReadWrapper(socket.recv))
```

@jbrockmendel `pytz` has a whole different thing going on, but UTC is so simple that basically all implementations will have the same properties. This version has some fast paths for `dateutil`-specific stuff (like checking ambiguities), but will work the same as `pytz.utc`. Of course, there's no way to make `pytz.utc is dateutil.tz.UTC` or `dateutil.tz.UTC is datetime.timezone.UTC`, but that's fine. This will mostly make it slightly cheaper to use UTC from `dateutil` (no need to construct a new instance or even call the constructor now), and move us down the road to a one-object-per-zone model.

From: Will Holmgren [mailto:notifications@github.com]
Sent: Friday, July 08, 2016 5:12 PM
To: pvlib/pvlib-python <pvlib-python@noreply.github.com>
Subject: [EXTERNAL] [pvlib/pvlib-python] Bug in extraterrestrial radiation calculation (#211)


The ET radiation calculator needs the "day angle" of the Earth's position in its orbit around the Sun. Our extraradiation function and the matlab pvl_extraradiation calculate the angle as:

B = (2. * pi / 365.) * doy

Assuming that doy is a value between 1 and 365, I believe it should be

B = (2. * pi / 365.) * (doy - 1)

The disc and pvl_disc functions use the (doy-1) formula.

In the implementation str.indexof creates clauses that use str.contains, but not the other way around.
In particular, str.indexof has to identify the _first_ occurrence of a substring. 
Positive occurrences of str.contains are easy: they expand into an equality (str.contains x y) expands into (str.concat y1 y y2) = x. Negative occurrences are handled by incremental unfolding. 
str.indexof, on the other hand, is compiled into a set of clauses. The method used for str.indexof with the setting string_solver=seq is currently incorrect as reported in #1397.

I agree clone_network is perfect for the case not share params.  let's focus on sharing params.

A example: there is a `layer1`, a `layer_contains_layer1`, `another_layer_contains_layer1`, `yet_another_layer_contains_layer1`, and `final_layer` is a layer contains `layer_contains_layer1` and `another_layer_contains_layer1`. like this:

> final_layer = lasagne.layers.ElemwiseMergeLayer((layer_contains_layer1,another_layer_contains_layer1),T.add)

now let's do this:

> final_layer2 = lasagne.layers.clone_network(final_layer, bottom_layers={another_layer_contains_layer1:yet_another_layer_contains_layer1},share_params=True)

The `another_layer_contains_layer1` in `final_layer` will be replaced by `yet_another_layer_contains_layer1` which contains a real layer1, other hand, `layer_contains_layer1` in `final_layer` will be cloned, that means the place of layer1 in this part of final_layer2 is a new layer that share params with layer1. final_layer2 contains layer1 and a clone of layer1 at same time, this looks not good.

The result I prefer is, `final_layer2` should keep as most layer same to `final_layer` as posible, because `final_layer` is "some concept of layer1", if we replace a part of `final_layer` with "another concept of layer1" the result `final_layer2` should still be "some concept of layer1".  "some concept of layer1" means we should be able do something like `lasagne.layers.join_layer(final_layer2,{layer1:layer2})`, that is by replace layer1 in final_layer2 with layer2 got a "some concept of layer2".

I managed to forward the MAVLink messages coming from an UAV connected to a serial port through DroneKit to QGroundControl.

UAV (PX4) <==== (serial) ====> DroneKit <==== (MAVLink UDP stream) ====> QGroundControl

The code to do so is already present in DroneKit. Look into the [`mavlink.py`](https://github.com/dronekit/dronekit-python/blob/master/dronekit/mavlink.py) file for the `mavudpin_multi` class and for the `MAVConnection.pipe()` method.

The code I used is:

```python
vehicle = dronekit.connect('/dev/ttyS0')  # ... and other connection options
udp_conn = MAVConnection('udpin:0.0.0.0:15667', source_system=1)
vehicle._handler.pipe(udp_conn)
udp_conn.master.mav.srcComponent = 1  # needed to make QGroundControl work!
udp_conn.start()
```

This code starts a listener on UDP port 15667 which automatically establishes the necessary connections with clients. All MAVLink messages coming from the vehicle are forwarded on the UDP clients, and all the messages coming from the UDP clients are forwarded to the client.

To make the forwarding work I had to alter the source component ID.

Then I configured QGroundControl to contact the host running DroneKit via UDP on port 15667. QGroundControl itself should listen on another port.
Here's an example configuration:

![QGroundControl configuration](https://i.imgur.com/5nJsfYD.png)

This solved the problem I had. In my React app I keep an eye on the `expires_at` that gets set in `localStorage`. If my code deems the access token too old, it triggers the same code path as was used to redirect to the kinto authorize endpoint **but** with `&prompt=none` added. 

If I try to go to `http://localhost:8888/v1/openid/auth0/login?callback=http%3A%2F%2Flocalhost%3A3000%2F%23provider%3Dauth0%26tokens%3D&scope=openid%20email%20profile&prompt=none` I get:
```
< HTTP/1.1 307 Temporary Redirect
< Location: https://peterbecom.auth0.com/authorize?client_id=R6R1t40cXQnuPQBdC8D83WOVyuUppZdw&response_type=code&scope=openid+email+profile&redirect_uri=http%3A%2F%2Flocalhost%3A8888%2Fv1%2Fopenid%2Fauth0%2Ftoken%3F&state=de6b3235872078a889f2c9134fe8c65c3167ee6819bf71289473570f8c927b74
```
However, with the patch in this PR, that HTTP response becomes this instead:
```
< HTTP/1.1 307 Temporary Redirect
< Location: https://peterbecom.auth0.com/authorize?client_id=R6R1t40cXQnuPQBdC8D83WOVyuUppZdw&response_type=code&scope=openid+email+profile&redirect_uri=http%3A%2F%2Flocalhost%3A8888%2Fv1%2Fopenid%2Fauth0%2Ftoken%3F&state=de6b3235872078a889f2c9134fe8c65c3167ee6819bf71289473570f8c927b74&prompt=none
```
The only difference is the extra `&prompt=none` at the end of the URL. Auth0 needs that so that when my browser arrives there at `https://peterbecom.auth0.com/authorize...` it doesn't ever present a 200 OK. Instead the only thing that can and should happen is that it redirects back to `http://localhost:8888/v1/openid/auth0/login` with a code or with an error.

I'll reopen this. There are currently a few deficiencies with the current config parser. The most glaring one is that this doesn't work as expected:

    collections = [
        ["foo", "bar"]
    ]

Because the second line starts with a space, it will be treated as a continuation line of the `collections` value. The third one, however, is not part of the value and yields a parsing error.

This is not really a shortcoming of the stdlib config parser, just the way we combine it with our custom JSON parsing.

Meanwhile https://github.com/toml-lang/toml/issues/271 has long been resolved. The parser above is still badly written, but [pytoml](https://github.com/avakar/pytoml) seems to be slightly better. We'll just have to try it out.

If you use `collections = null`, vdirsyncer disables a lot of sanity checks to see if the thing you're pointing it to is actually a valid collection. Originally it was the only option when vdirsyncer could only sync a single collection per pair, but it is nowadays intended as a workaround to be used with DAV-servers which don't implement the protocols for discovering collections properly.

I am actually surprised that `PreconditionFailed` is shown in this case, because that usually indicates a data race that has been prevented (a very unlikely edge case that only happens if two clients sync large amounts of data at the same time. Even in that situation it is as likely as an accidental hash collision).

Here is the interface I have in mind:

- start `raiden` command with arguments
 * `eth_rpc_host`
 * `eth_rpc_port`
 * `eth_privkey`
 * `raiden_registry_contract_address`
 * `raiden_discovery_contract_address`
 * `raiden_privkey`
 * `raiden_host=0.0.0.0` 
 * `raiden_port=40001`
 * `raiden_external_host`
 * `raiden_external_port`
- this starts the raiden node and
 * ensures that `*_contract_address` exists
 * registers/updates the `(raiden_external_host, raiden_external_port)` with `ethereum.utils.privtoaddr(raiden_privkey)` at `raiden_discovery_address`
 * enters a REPL with the following `locals`
   * `raiden_cli`
     * property: `blockchain` - the [BlockChainService](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/master/raiden/network/rpc/client.py#L49)
      * function: `find_peer_by_endpoint(host, port)` -> `address`
      * function: `find_peer_by_address(address)` -> `(host, port)`

shortcomings:
 - `raiden_external_host` and `raiden_external_port` need to be determined by the user

TODOs:
- `BlockChainService` methods are incomplete: https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/master/raiden/network/rpc/client.py#L124-L166
- `raiden_discovery_contract` is not implemented yet

Thanks @stonecoldpat for bringing the issue up.

> but some other things should be put in its place.

We don't necessarily need an alternative approach, it's sub optimal for a cooperative scenario to go through the dispute on-chain, but it will have the same netted value as a cooperative close (or another method).

> I think the nonce should still be preserved to uniquely identify channels

The nonces are important both for the smart contract and at the protocol level.

Protocol: We are not assuming a transport protocol with reliable delivery, in fact the current implementation is using UDP as a transport, the nonces are used at the protocol level for error detection, this means that the current raiden implementation requires a sequential nonce for each message.

Smart Contract: Nonces are required to order messages provided by third parties #293 because a third party might be lagging behind, for this we only need a monotonically increasing value, so an implementation using transport with reliable delivery can use nonces increasing at different steps (for whatever reason).

> your smart contract might allow Alice and Bob to open two or more channels at the same time. *(of course; this might apply if the Raiden contract is deployed twice on Ethereum. Two A->B channels can be opened at the same time in this instance. )

Yes, indeed this can happen **now**, we have an issue for replay attacks mitigation #292 , there is nothing preventing someone to deploy a different registry, add the same tokens in it, and open channels at the same block height, the rationale apply for different blockchains too.

> So what I propose (this will need discussion) is that the nonce is uniquely identifiable to the channel itself

Since we use the nonce to order messages provided by third parties, we are considering to use the message signature with additional data that is contract specific #43 (this scheme will change as we discuss #292 , at least we also need blockchain specific data).

As for todos, after removing the courtesy argument, we should also rename `updateTransfer` to something more meaningful, my first suggestion `counterpartyClose`.
